metadata.title: The Pareto Frontier: Finding the Cheapest Model That Solves Your Task

beats.narration_text: Claude Opus is smartest but costs five times more. Haiku is cheap. You have no idea which one makes sense for your task. This is Liam, in for Bear.

props.segment: The Pareto Frontier: Finding the Cheapest Model That Solves Your Task
props.command: I need to pick between Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku for my task. Opus is 5× more expensive but smarter. Which one sits on the pareto frontier for my use case?

beats.narration_text: The question: how do you know which model to use for a task — without trying them all and paying for them all?

Run the same eval suite against multiple models. Plot quality — accuracy percentage — against cost in dollars. The pareto frontier is the curve of non-dominated points. A point is on the frontier if you cannot improve accuracy without paying more, or paying more without improving accuracy. Your task's optimal model sits on that frontier — not at the top of the accuracy axis, not at the bottom of the cost axis. On the frontier.

Here is the scatter plot. Customer-support classification task. X axis: cost per call. Y axis: accuracy. Three dots. Opus: 98% accuracy at eight cents per call. Haiku: 82% accuracy at one cent per call. Sonnet: 90% accuracy at four cents per call. Draw the frontier. It passes through Sonnet. Opus is above the frontier — you can get 90% for half the price. Haiku is below — you lose 8 points of accuracy to save three cents. Sonnet is the rational choice. 90% is good enough for this task. You save four cents per call. Across 100,000 calls: four thousand dollars back in your budget.

The accumulation is how you find the frontier. Run the same eval across all models. Record accuracy, cost, and latency per cell. Plot. The frontier draws itself. The model that wins is not always obvious before the sweep — Sonnet was not obviously the right answer. The sweep proved it.

Before you can plot the frontier, you need the numbers. Here is the cost table. Opus: fifteen dollars per million input tokens, seventy-five dollars per million output tokens. Sonnet: three dollars in, fifteen dollars out. Haiku: twenty-five cents in, one dollar twenty-five out. Quality scores on the right — 98, 90, and 82 percent respectively, measured against a real eval suite. Haiku is the cheapest — highlighted in green because cheap is not bad, it is the starting point. Opus gets the terracotta accent because it is the reference ceiling: the best quality you can buy. Sonnet sits between them — and in this case, it is also on the frontier. These numbers are relative. Always check current pricing before you build. Cost is a variable, not a constraint.

props.sparkLine: Cost is a variable, not a constraint.

beats.narration_text: Here is how you read the frontier. X axis: cost per call, increasing right. Y axis: accuracy, increasing up. Opus sits high and right — expensive, accurate. Haiku sits low and left — cheap, less accurate. Sonnet sits in the middle. The dashed frontier line passes through the non-dominated points. Points below and left of the frontier are dominated — you could pay the same cost and get more accuracy, or get the same accuracy for less. The terracotta ring marks the frontier point. Your decision rule: find the point on the frontier closest to your accuracy threshold. If you need 90 percent, Sonnet is your answer. If you can accept 82 percent, Haiku saves you three cents per call. If you need 95, Opus is the only option. The frontier does not make the decision for you — it shows you where the real trade-off lives.

props.sparkLine: On the frontier — never off it.

beats.narration_text: The sweep in practice is five steps, left to right. One: build your eval suite — ten representative tasks that reflect real usage. Two: run all three models against the same suite. Three: collect the quality and cost pair for each model. Four: plot the scatter. This is where the decision happens — the data takes a shape, and the frontier becomes visible. Five: pick the frontier model for your quality threshold. Each step has a code equivalent: the loop is three lines, the plot is one function call. The sweep is not an expensive research project. It is a script you run once before you commit to a model — and run again whenever pricing or quality shifts.

props.sparkLine: The sweep is three lines of code.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Run the same eval suite across Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku. Plot accuracy against cost. The pareto frontier is the curve of models where you cannot improve one without sacrificing the other. Your task-optimal model is on that frontier. For a 90%-threshold classification task: Sonnet at four cents per call, saving four thousand dollars per hundred thousand calls over Opus.

artifactLines: Run the same eval against Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku — record accuracy, cost, and latency.
The pareto frontier: models where you cannot improve accuracy without paying more.
Your task-optimal model sits on the frontier — not necessarily the most accurate.
Sonnet at 90% accuracy, $0.04/call: $4,000 saved per 100,000 calls vs. Opus.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here is the prompt: 'Sweep my task across Opus, Sonnet, and Haiku, plot cost versus accuracy, and tell me which model sits on the pareto frontier for me.' What you are building is the sweep harness — the loop that runs the same eval task against each model, records accuracy and cost per cell, and plots the result. Watch where your task's threshold falls on the accuracy axis: if 85% is good enough, Haiku might be your frontier model, not Sonnet. If you need 95%, Opus might be the only option. The frontier is task-specific. The sweep is what reveals it. Take this prompt, run it on your own task, and find where your dot lands. Liam, in for Bear.
